subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Comprehensive, labelled table of contents and clear section numbering
Explicit introductionâbodyâconclusion structure with captions and bibliography header
Strong overall coherence and logical progression of the mathematical narrative
weaknesses: Occasional digressions and redundant anecdotal material reduce conciseness
Inconsistent caption formatting and footnote citation style
Conclusion lacks a concise summary of key findings
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is well organized and coherent, with excellent structure (TOC, numbered sections, bibliography). However, the repetitive narrative and formatting inconsistencies prevent it from achieving top-band conciseness. A score of 3 reflects strong organization and logical flow but room for improved conciseness and consistency.

strengths: Mostly consistent and accurate use of standard calculus notation
Effective combination of equations, verbal explanations and diagrams
Clear logical structure in the step-by-step proofs
weaknesses: Minor notational slips (e.g. missing negative sign, unclear Î notation)
Disconnected sourced images lacking mathematical commentary
A few formatting errors in final evaluation steps
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematical language and multiple representations are used effectively, and the logical structure is clear. Occasional notation errors and under-annotated figures prevent a topâband score. A 3 acknowledges generally strong communication with minor lapses.

strengths: Personal narrative around first encountering the painterâs paradox shows clear voice
Insightful real-world extension linking Gabrielâs Horn to acoustics
weaknesses: Investigative approaches follow standard textbook derivations without significant innovation
Limited creative adaptation beyond topic selection
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: There is clear evidence of independent thought and a personal approach, but the mathematics itself follows conventional proofs with limited originality. A mid-band score of 2 appropriately reflects significant, though not outstanding, engagement.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on initial hypothesis and why intuition was misleading
Thoughtful analysis of key steps (e.g. removing squareâroot term)
Clear evaluation of how results resolve the painterâs paradox
weaknesses: Reflection lacks deep critical insight and precise, testable hypotheses
Future implications are mentioned only briefly
Could strengthen connection between results and broader implications
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student provides meaningful reflection and thoughtful analysis, but the depth and critical insight fall short of the top band. A score of 2 reflects consistent reflection with room for more substantial critique and future-oriented evaluation.

strengths: Appropriate and relevant use of improper-integral methods for volume and surface area
Clear conceptual understanding and correct limits in integrals
Thoughtful comparison of shell and disc methods
weaknesses: Minor accuracy issues (missing dx in surface-area formula, derivative sign error)
Scope of mathematics is narrow, confined to standard proofs
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The mathematics used is relevant and shows good understanding, but minor errors in notation and limited scope prevent a higher mark. A score of 2 reflects appropriate application with some inaccuracies and a need for broader mathematical depth.

strandSelections.reasoning: The proofs are broken into labelled steps and the algebra is shown sequentially, giving a clear logical path.  Occasional jumps (e.g. discarding the square-root term to argue divergence) slightly weaken rigour, but overall clarity remains good, matching the âgood logical structureâ descriptor.

The student shows good conceptual understanding of the volume integral and uses correct limits.  Minor errors (sign of derivative, informal justification for removing the square root) introduce some inaccuracy, so âgood understanding with minor errorsâ is the fairest assessment.

Equations, verbal explanations and several diagrams (function graph, real-life statue, frustum sketch) are combined sensibly.  They help the reader follow the argument, but the representations are relatively simple and sometimes sourced images are used without further mathematical annotation, so the strand sits comfortably in the âeffectiveâ rather than âhighly effectiveâ band.

The exploration follows a clear narrative: introduction of the paradox, historical background, development of the volume proof, development of the surfaceâarea proof and a short conclusion.  While occasional digressions (e.g. lengthy personal anecdotes and physics comments) interrupt the flow, the mathematical argument still progresses logically from definitions to results, so overall coherence is good but not outstanding.

Beyond selecting the topic, there is limited evidence of creative mathematical adaptation or investigative initiative.  The derivations mirror standard textbook proofs without substantial modification, fulfilling âsome creativity or initiativeâ but not reaching the higher descriptors.

Results are compared to the hypothesis and the student comments on the logical step of dropping the square root to establish divergence.  This constitutes a thoughtful, though not penetrating, analysis of process and outcome.

The conclusion explains how the findings resolve the painterâs paradox and briefly relates the idea to acoustics, satisfying the rubric language of a âclear evaluation of outcomes and implications.â  The discussion of future possibilities is limited, so it does not meet the highest band.

The student states an initial hypothesis, revisits it in the conclusion and discusses why intuition was misleading, showing meaningful reflection throughout.  The discussion is not deeply critical, so it fits the âmeaningfulâ level rather than âsubstantialâ.

Standard calculus notation (improper-integral symbols, f(x), fâ²(x), Ï) is used consistently and variables are defined.  A few inaccuracies appear (e.g. writing fâ²(x)=1/xÂ² instead of â1/xÂ²), but these are minor slips rather than systematic misuse, so the language is âmostly consistentâ rather than perfect.

The narrative of first encountering the concept on social media and linking it to future studies gives the work a recognizable personal voice, and the student selects which integration method to use.  However, the approach remains fairly conventional, so the middle band is most appropriate.

Although most of the writing is relevant to Gabrielâs Horn, the student repeatedly restates basic ideas (e.g. the painterâs paradox) and includes superfluous narrative about internet searches and acoustics.  This redundancy means the text is not truly concise, so the work aligns best with âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elements.â

The improper integral for volume and the surface-area integral are fully relevant to the problem and used appropriately.  No extraneous procedures are introduced, so the work meets the criteria for ârelevant mathematics used appropriately,â but its scope is narrow, preventing a higher band.

Choosing the classic paradox and voicing a personal hypothesis shows some independent thought beyond textbook repetition.  Nevertheless, the mathematics follows well-known textbook derivations with little original extension, so the evidence is âsignificantâ rather than âoutstandingâ.

The work contains a labelled table of contents, numbered sections and subsections, figures with captions and a final bibliography, giving the reader a very clear map of the document.  The introduction/body/conclusion structure is explicit and easy to follow, so the organisation satisfies the description of the top band for this strand.

Improper integrals and surface-area formulae are explicitly on the HL syllabus and are applied at an expected depth, demonstrating mathematics commensurate with course level but not extending beyond it, aligning with the 3-mark band.

annotations.comment: The table of contents is comprehensive, with clear section numbering and page references, supporting easy navigation.
The paragraph describing figure 1 lacks a mathematical commentary on how the diagram supports the argument. Add an equation or descriptive sentence linking the figure to the integral setup.
The title clearly states the research question and effectively engages the reader with the paradoxical theme.
Splitting the title over two lines interrupts readability. Consider combining it into a single line or using a subtitle format for coherence.
Consider adding dot leaders between section titles and page numbers to improve visual alignment and conciseness.
The introductory anecdote is engaging but overly repetitious about the studentâs discovery process. Streamline this narrative to focus more quickly on the mathematics.
There are several misspellings (e.g. âGaberialâs,â âGarielâsâ). Review carefully to ensure accurate mathematical terminology.
The infinitesimal element dx is omitted from the surface area formula. It should read $$dS = 2\pi f(x)\sqrt{1+f'(x)^2}\,dx$$ for mathematical accuracy.
Introduce the formula for the lateral surface area of a frustum when you discuss the net; this will clarify how the integral arises.
Figure 1 caption formatting is inconsistent with later figures. Standardize caption numbering and placement to improve presentation.
The realâlife photograph is visually appealing but disconnected from the mathematical discussion. Add commentary on how it illustrates the analytic properties of the horn.
The hypothesis is stated in narrative form but lacks a precise mathematical prediction. Refine it to a clear, testable statement about the expected integral result.
The explanation comparing shell and disc methods is clear and demonstrates thoughtful choice of technique.
Citation in footnote 7 appears as â\frac{7}{7} (Maltby et al.)â; revise to proper authorâyear or numbered style to meet academic conventions.
In the derivation of ÎL, the notation âÎf/Î dxâ is unclear. Replace with (Îf/Îx) or fâ²(x) to maintain consistent calculus notation.
The substitution discussion spans many lines. Consider summarizing the rationale and using a single displayed equation to improve conciseness.
The description of the painterâs paradox in the background section conveys personal insight into your thought process.
The final evaluation step is misformatted as â=Ï[1]Ï.â It should clearly show $$\pi\Bigl(-\tfrac1x\Bigr)\Big|_1^\infty = \pi$$.
After finding the volume equals \(\pi\), explicitly connect this to resolving the painterâs paradox in one or two sentences to strengthen reflection.
The bibliography header is clearly marked, making it easy for the reader to locate references.
Long lists in the final bibliography span pages. Consider merging into one alphabetical list and removing duplicate entries.
Include full DOI formatting (e.g. âhttps://doi.org/â¦â) rather than plain URLs to meet academic standards.
The derivative is given as \(f'(x)=1/x^2\) but should be \(f'(x)=-1/x^2\). Acknowledge the negative sign even if it vanishes when squared.
When removing the square root term to prove divergence, include a concise justification using the inequality \(\sqrt{1+1/x^4}>1\).
The conclusion section spans multiple pages without a clear summary. Restructure to provide a concise recap of key findings before applications.
Linking Gabrielâs Horn to acoustics provides an insightful real-world extension beyond the syllabus.
Citation formatting varies widely in the list. Adopt a single consistent style (e.g. APA or MLA) to improve professionalism.
